content:
The vault had not gotten warmer. Seven days of reading the stat journals had not acclimatized me to the cold, or to the smell, or to the weight of the stone pressing against my spine as I sat hunched among the stacks. If anything, the vault felt heavier now, as though the accumulated dread of a million years had its own gravitational mass. My father's final words to me echoed in the hollow space behind my sternum. He had said the stat journals were mandatory. He had said the superpower journals were worse. He had paused after saying that, his papery lips pressing together, and then he had said nothing else, because there was nothing else to say. The silence had been the warning.

I had not believed him. After reading Harlan's account of Godric's strength obliterating the omniverse from a relaxed shoulder, after Daven's record of the Worldspine's fist disintegrating against an open palm, after Merric's meticulous breakdown of probability itself serving Godric like a trained dog, I had believed I had reached the floor of the abyss. I believed the stat journals represented the bedrock of horror. I was wrong. The stat journals were the surface. The superpower journals were what lay beneath the surface, in the lightless, crushing dark where language failed and mathematics died.

I reached for the first superpower journal. The binding was stiff, the leather cracked and dark. The handwriting inside was cramped, precise, each letter formed with the deliberate control of a man fighting to keep his hand steady through sheer professional discipline.

**Year 673,889 — Perrin — Superpower: Absolute Molecular Dissolution**

I am recording this incident in the format of a formal diagnostic report because I am a diagnostician by nature and training, and if I do not impose structure upon what I witnessed, my mind will abandon structure entirely and I will never retrieve it. I have served as Lord Godric's court wizard for twenty-three years. My magical perception, amplified one thousandfold daily since my twentieth birthday, allows me to observe and catalog phenomena at the subatomic level across multiversal distances. I am, by any standard the omniverse recognizes, a being of extraordinary analytical capability. What I observed today exceeded my capacity to catalog by a margin so vast that the word exceeded is an insult to the scale of my inadequacy.

The opposition was the Eternity Lattice. They were not an army or a pantheon. They were a structure. A civilization of sentient, indestructible crystalline matter spanning six full multiversal clusters, comprising decillions of universes, each universe containing googolplexian galaxies. The Lattice existed as a single, interconnected organism of hyper-dense molecular bonds so absolute that the energy required to break a single bond exceeded the total energy output of a dying omniverse. They were, by the metrics of physical science, the most indestructible material entity in existence. No force, no energy, no magic, no conceptual weapon had ever successfully severed even one of their molecular bonds. Their invulnerability was not a defense. It was their identity. They existed because they could not be broken.

They opposed Lord Godric because they calculated that their structural indestructibility rendered them immune to physical annihilation. They calculated that Godric, whose power was physical in nature, could not dissolve what could not be dissolved. The Weakness Paradox, of course, had engaged the moment this thought formed in their collective consciousness thousands of years prior. Godric had already passively acquired absolute immunity to molecular-bond-based invulnerability and perfect mastery over the concept of structural dissolution. The Lattice's preparation had, as always, merely fed the paradox, triggering the evolved variant, which had injected additional devastating effects into this specific superpower long before the Lattice arrived.

Godric told me he wanted to test a superpower. He called it Absolute Molecular Dissolution. He described it, in his warm, conversational baritone, as the ability to dissolve the molecular bonds of any target. He mentioned this the way a man might mention he was considering trying a new recipe for dinner. I asked him if he was concerned about the Lattice's indestructibility. He looked at me with genuine, puzzled innocence and asked what indestructibility meant in this context.

The Lattice manifested above the capital world as a wall of screaming, hyper-dense crystal stretching across the horizon of perceivable space. The sky was replaced. The stars were replaced. Everything above us became the Lattice, a solid plane of absolute molecular integrity so dense that its gravitational presence began to compress the orbital paths of the capital's moons into decaying spirals. The sheer physical weight of their presence cracked the planetary crust beneath my boots. I reinforced my personal wards with the full, staggering output of my amplified mana and still felt my ribs creak under the pressure.

Godric was seated on a stone bench in the garden courtyard. He had been examining the root structure of a newly planted olive tree. He did not stand. He did not look up at the wall of indestructible crystal that had replaced the sky. He exhaled. A slow, relaxed breath through his nose, the kind of breath a man takes when he is leaning back in a chair and deciding whether to close his eyes for a moment.

The exhalation carried with it, at effectively zero percent of its true potency, the superpower of Absolute Molecular Dissolution. The evolved variant of this power, amplified countless times by the Evolved Weakness Paradox, did not merely dissolve molecular bonds. It dissolved the concept of bonds. It unmade the relationships between particles. It severed the connections that allowed matter to recognize itself as matter.

The Lattice's hyper-dense crystalline structure, the most indestructible material in the omniverse, did not crack. It did not shatter. Shattering implies that the bonds were broken by force. There was no force. The bonds simply ceased to exist. The relationship between the atoms, the fundamental agreement that held them together as a solid object, was deleted from the physics of reality. The crystal did not fall apart. It forgot how to be solid.

The dissolution began at the leading edge of the Lattice, the point closest to Godric's exhaled breath, and propagated outward at a velocity that made the speed of light look like a geological process. I watched through my amplified magical perception as the hyper-dense molecular bonds, each one containing more energy than a collapsing omniverse, simply vanished. The crystal did not melt. It did not sublimate. The atoms, freed from their bonds, retained their positions for one infinitesimal fraction of a Planck second, hovering in the shape of the structure they had once formed, like a photograph of a building whose mortar had been removed. Then the ambient thermal energy of the surrounding space caused the free atoms to move.

The result was catastrophic on a scale that my vocabulary cannot adequately convey. Six full multiversal clusters, each containing decillions of universes, each universe containing googolplexian galaxies, underwent simultaneous molecular dissolution. The Lattice's structure propagated the dissolution through its own body like a plague, each dissolved bond releasing its stored energy into the surrounding space, which accelerated the dissolution of the next bond, which released more energy. It was a chain reaction, but the word chain reaction implies a linear progression. This was not linear. The evolved superpower had mutated the effect into an omnidirectional cascade. The dissolution propagated forward, backward, sideways, across dimensional boundaries, through temporal layers, and into conceptual spaces that I did not have the sensory apparatus to perceive. Every direction was simultaneously the direction of destruction.

The physical consequences were grotesque beyond description. The crystalline entities that comprised the Lattice, each one a sentient being of indestructible molecular architecture, experienced the dissolution of their bodies as a visceral, biological catastrophe. Their crystalline forms, which had never possessed biology, were forced by the evolved superpower to experience the sensation of biological dissolution. Their molecular structure came apart in wet, organic-sounding cracks, each severed bond producing a sound like bone splitting inside a living body. Their crystalline skin peeled away in thin, translucent sheets that dissolved into atomic mist before they hit the ground. Their internal structures, hyper-dense lattices of organized energy, collapsed inward, the energy dispersing in violent bursts that flash-boiled the surrounding space into superheated plasma. The entities screamed. They had never screamed before. They had never possessed the anatomy to scream. The evolved superpower had granted them the capacity to scream purely so they could express the agony of their unmaking.

The screaming was not sound. It was a vibration in the molecular structure of reality itself, a frequency of suffering so intense that it propagated through the dissolved matter and into the adjacent realities. Beings in universes light-years away from the Lattice's domain felt the vibration in their own molecular bonds. Their atoms trembled. In decillions of universes, in googolplexian galaxies, the inhabitants experienced a sudden, inexplicable terror as their own molecular structures sympathetically resonated with the dying screams of the Lattice. In the most affected regions, the sympathetic resonance was strong enough to loosen molecular bonds. Buildings collapsed. Mountains slumped. The bones of living beings became slightly less solid, and a percentage of the population across decillions of universes experienced spontaneous molecular failure, their bodies simply coming apart at the atomic level, their cells separating, their proteins unfolding, their DNA unzipping into free-floating nucleotides that pooled in their body cavities as a clear, viscous fluid. They did not die quickly. They remained conscious, their neural tissue being the last to dissolve, watching their own bodies come apart at the molecular level, feeling every bond sever, experiencing the sensation of being unmade from the inside out.

The collateral damage was not omniversal. It was worse. The dissolution cascade, amplified by the evolved superpower, did not stop at the Lattice's domain. It propagated through the dimensional boundaries and into the surrounding multiversal clusters, dissolving the molecular bonds of reality itself. Space-time lost its structural integrity. The fabric of existence, which depends on the consistent relationship between particles to maintain its physical properties, began to come apart. Universes did not explode or collapse. They dissolved. The matter within them lost cohesion, atom separating from atom, the organized structures of reality reducing to a uniform sea of free-floating particles that had no relationship to one another and therefore no physical properties. The sea expanded. Decillions of universes became oceans of dissociated atoms. The inhabitants of those universes, their molecular bonds dissolved, became clouds of free-floating organic particles that retained a terrible, flickering consciousness, aware that they were no longer beings, aware that they were now nothing more than dispersed atoms drifting in an endless, dark soup of former reality.

Godric closed his eyes. The dissolution stopped. Instantly, absolutely, as though a faucet had been turned off. The cascade, which had been consuming the omniverse at a velocity beyond measurement, simply ceased. The free-floating atoms, no longer targeted by the superpower, retained their dissociated state. The damage was done. The dissolved universes remained as oceans of disconnected particles, their former inhabitants still conscious, still suffering, still aware of their own atomic dispersal.

Godric opened his eyes. He looked at the sky, which was now empty, the Lattice entirely dissolved. He looked at the space where six multiversal clusters had been, now reduced to a uniform void of dissociated matter. He frowned. It was a small, mildly displeased frown, the expression of a man who had spilled a drop of soup on his shirt.

"That was more than I intended," he murmured. He rubbed his jaw with one calloused hand.

In zero seconds, he restored the dissolved universes. Every atom was reunited with its proper bonds. Every structure was rebuilt. Every being was reassembled, their consciousness restored, their bodies intact. But the memory remained. Every restored being across decillions of universes remembered, with perfect, excruciating clarity, the experience of being molecularly dissolved. They remembered the sensation of their bonds severing. They remembered the feeling of their bodies coming apart. They remembered the terror of floating as disconnected atoms in a void of former reality. The memory would haunt them for the remainder of their existence.

The Lattice was not restored. The crystalline entities, their indestructible molecular bonds dissolved, existed now as a fine, atomic dust suspended in a pocket dimension Godric had designated for their eternal containment. They were conscious. They could feel. They could perceive. But they had no bonds. They had no structure. They were nothing but dissociated atoms, each one individually aware, each one individually suffering, each one individually remembering what it had been to be indestructible, and knowing, with absolute certainty, that they would never be solid again.

Godric stood from the bench. He walked to the olive tree, crouched, and examined its roots again. He looked up at me, his face warm, his eyes crinkling with genuine concern.

"Perrin," he said, "you look unwell. The dust from the excavation may be irritating your lungs. I will have the gardeners water the soil more thoroughly." He paused, then added, almost as an afterthought, "I hope I did not startle you. I was only exhaling."

I did not speak. I could not. I stood in the garden, my amplified body trembling, my thousandfold-multiplied magic feeling like a child's toy in the shadow of a power that could dissolve the molecular bonds of the entire omniverse with an exhaled breath. I recorded this report because structure is the only thing keeping me functional. The structure is failing. I can feel it failing. But I will hold it together, because my King needs his court wizard, and because the alternative is to dissolve, and I have seen what dissolution looks like, and I will never, as long as I draw breath, allow myself to be anything other than whole in his presence.

---

I pressed my back against the vault wall and breathed through my nose. My hands were slick with sweat. The journal's pages were damp where my fingers had gripped them. Molecular dissolution. The concept of bonds deleted from physics. Six multiversal clusters reduced to oceans of dissociated, conscious atoms. An exhaled breath. He had been examining an olive tree. I pressed the heels of my palms against my eyes until the pressure spots burst into colored light, then reached for the next journal. The leather was softer, newer. The handwriting inside was loose, flowing, almost conversational, the letters rounded and youthful.

**Year 812,334 — Nolan — Superpower: Omniversal Flesh Transmutation**

I need to say something before I describe what happened. I need to say that I loved my King before this happened, and I love him now, and I will love him tomorrow. I need to say this first because what follows will make it very difficult to believe that a man who witnessed what I witnessed could still love the being responsible for it. But I do. He is kind to me. He remembers my children's names. He asked me, last week, whether my youngest son was enjoying his arcane studies, and his voice carried the genuine, invested warmth of a grandfather. I love him. I need you to understand that before you read what comes next, because if you do not understand that, you will think I am writing this out of hatred or resentment. I am not. I am writing this out of a need so fundamental that it transcends emotion. I am writing this because if I do not put the horror into words, the horror will put itself into my body, and I will not survive the translation.

The Ascended Geometry. That was the name of the opposition. They were not beings. They were proofs. Living mathematical theorems that had achieved consciousness and then transcended consciousness to become pure, perfect, invulnerable geometric forms. They existed as living equations, their bodies composed of absolute mathematical certainty. They had no biology. They had no flesh. They had no physical vulnerability of any kind, because they were not physical. They were the abstract made manifest, the theoretical made indestructible. They had spent a hundred thousand years constructing what they called the Apex Theorem, a mathematical proof that demonstrated, with absolute logical rigor, that no force in the omniverse could alter their fundamental nature. They were proof against transformation. They were proof against change. They were proof against everything.

They came to Thronegate because their theorem told them they could not be harmed. The Weakness Paradox had, of course, engaged the instant the first theorem-entity conceived the thought that mathematical invulnerability could serve as a defense against Godric. The evolved paradox had already injected multiple devastating enhanced effects into the superpower Godric intended to test. The paradox had also permanently amplified Godric's already infinite power, though the increase, relative to his baseline, was so infinitesimal as to be effectively zero, a rounding error so small that the omniverse itself could not measure it. But for any other entity, for any god or titan or cosmic force, the amplification granted by this single paradox trigger would have elevated them from mortality to omnipotence a trillion times over. For Godric, it was nothing. It was less than nothing. It was a speck of dust landing on a mountain that was already infinitely taller than itself.

Godric told me he wanted to test a superpower called Omniversal Flesh Transmutation. He described it, over a quiet breakfast in the palace kitchen, as the ability to convert any substance, energy, or concept into living biological flesh. He said it with the same casual tone he used when describing the weather. I asked him if the Ascended Geometry's mathematical invulnerability concerned him. He chewed slowly, considered the question, and said he did not think it would be a problem.

The Geometry manifested in the grand hall of the citadel. They arrived not as physical beings but as mathematical assertions, rewriting the local physics to accommodate their presence. The hall filled with impossible shapes, living polyhedra of pure logical certainty, their surfaces radiating the cold, absolute light of mathematical proof. The air itself became a theorem, every molecule in the room suddenly certain of its own invulnerability. My amplified wards, constructed from twenty-three years of thousandfold daily magical amplification, were not destroyed by the Geometry's presence. They were proven unnecessary. The Geometry's theorem demonstrated, with irrefutable logic, that my wards could not be needed, because harm was mathematically impossible in their presence. My wards simply deactivated, their magical structure conceding to the superior logic.

Godric was sitting at the far end of the hall, in a simple wooden chair, reviewing a document. He did not look up when the Geometry arrived. He did not look up when their theorem rewrote the local physics. He cleared his throat. A small, reflexive action, the kind of thing a person does when they have been reading for a while and their throat feels dry.

The throat-clearing carried, at effectively zero percent of its true potency, the superpower of Omniversal Flesh Transmutation. The evolved variant of this power, amplified countless times beyond its already omniverse-destroying base, did not merely convert matter into flesh. It converted the fundamental nature of things into flesh. It took the abstract, the mathematical, the conceptual, the impossible, and forced it to become biological. The transmutation did not ask permission. It did not negotiate with the target's existing nature. It simply overwrote whatever the target was with flesh, and the flesh was alive, and the flesh was conscious, and the flesh could suffer.

The Ascended Geometry's mathematical invulnerability, their absolute proof against transformation, lasted approximately zero seconds against the transmutation. The theorem that protected them was itself converted into flesh. The mathematical certainty of their invulnerability became a biological organ, a pulsing, wet lump of gray matter that throbbed in the center of each transmuted form, still attempting to compute proofs but now doing so through biological neurons that fired in agonizing, misfiring cascades. The living polyhedra, the pure geometric forms of absolute mathematical perfection, were instantaneously and violently forced into biological existence.

The transformation was not gentle. It was not gradual. It was a catastrophic ontological rupture, the abstract being dragged into the physical with the subtlety of a sledgehammer. Their geometric surfaces, which had never possessed texture, erupted in wet, glistening membranes of living tissue. The transition from pure mathematics to raw biology was so abrupt that the newly formed flesh did not have time to organize itself into coherent anatomy. The first layer was a screaming, undifferentiated mass of cells, a biological chaos of muscle, nerve, and epithelium that writhed and contracted without structure or purpose. Then the evolved superpower imposed organization, and the organization was horror.

Each former geometric entity became a towering column of living flesh, but the flesh was wrong. The evolved superpower had taken the mathematical properties of each entity and translated them into biological equivalents. An entity that had existed as a perfect sphere became a sphere of flesh, but the flesh was composed entirely of exposed nerve endings, packed so densely that the sphere was more pain receptor than tissue. An entity that had existed as a tesseract, a four-dimensional hypercube, was forced into three-dimensional biology, and the compression of its fourth dimension into flesh caused it to fold inward on itself, its organs turning inside out, its digestive tract becoming external, its skin becoming intestinal lining that wept acidic mucus onto the floor. An entity that had existed as an infinite fractal was forced into finite biological form, and the compression of its infinite complexity into a finite body caused the flesh to proliferate uncontrollably, a cancerous explosion of tissue that grew and grew, sprouting eyes, mouths, and grasping hands from every surface, each one fully functional and each one screaming.

The screaming. I must describe the screaming. These entities had never possessed vocal anatomy. They had never produced sound. The evolved superpower had granted them mouths, throats, lungs, and vocal cords purely so they could scream. And they screamed. The sound was not merely loud. It was biologically impossible, a frequency of agony that exceeded the structural tolerance of the air itself. The sound waves cracked the marble floor. They shattered the stained glass. They ruptured my eardrums, and I felt hot blood running down my neck from both ears. The screaming propagated through the dimensional boundaries of the citadel and out into the surrounding cosmos. The sound carried the transmutation's resonance. Every reality the screaming touched experienced a faint, diluted echo of the flesh transmutation. In googolplexian galaxies across decillions of universes, inanimate objects spontaneously developed rudimentary biology. Stone walls grew thin, veiny membranes that pulsed with borrowed life. Metal surfaces sprouted coarse, black hairs that writhed like worms. Water became viscous, acquiring a faint, biological warmth and a faint, biological smell. The inhabitants of these galaxies did not die, but they were traumatized on a fundamental level, watching their environments twitch and breathe around them, their furniture growing soft and warm, their tools developing pulse rates.

The transmutation cascade did not stop at the Geometry. The evolved superpower had built in a contagion effect. The newly created flesh, the screaming columns of transmuted mathematics, began to spread. The flesh grew, extended, reached outward with grasping tendrils of wet, red tissue. Wherever the tendrils touched, the matter was converted into more flesh. The marble floor of the grand hall became a pulsing carpet of living tissue. The stone walls became walls of muscle, contracting and relaxing in a slow, rhythmic peristalsis. The wooden furniture sprouted bones. The air itself acquired a thin, mucous quality, as though the atmosphere was being slowly digested by the growing biological mass.

The contagion reached the citadel's exterior and continued. The planetary surface beneath the citadel underwent transmutation. Continents of rock became continents of flesh, mountain ranges becoming ridges of bone covered in taut, wet skin, oceans becoming vast, warm lakes of biological fluid that smelled of copper and lymph. The transmutation propagated outward from the planet, reaching the orbital stations, the moon bases, the mining colonies in the asteroid belts. Every inanimate object, every structure, every machine was converted into living, conscious flesh. The mechanical infrastructure of the capital system, the product of a million years of engineering, became a screaming, writhing biological mass in less than a second.

And it kept going. The contagion crossed interstellar distances, propagating through the vacuum of space itself, converting the interstellar medium into a thin, diffuse biological film that stretched between stars like a web of living tissue. It reached neighboring star systems. It converted planets, moons, and stellar bodies. A gas giant became a colossal, spherical mass of warm, pulsing flesh, its atmosphere a breathing membrane of living gas that sighed with biological contentment. A neutron star, its hyper-dense matter the most compressed substance in the physical universe, was forced into biological form, and the compression of its impossible density into cellular structure produced flesh so dense that each cell contained the mass of a continent. The newly biological neutron star screamed in a frequency that shattered the dimensional walls of the local multiversal cluster.

The collateral damage was absolute. The transmutation contagion, amplified by the evolved superpower, propagated across the entire omniverse. Every reality, every timeline, every dimension, every plane of existence was touched. The omniverse did not die. It was converted. The entire omniverse, the absolute container of all existence, was transformed from a structure of physics and mathematics into a structure of living, conscious, suffering flesh. Every star became a biological organ. Every planet became a growth of tissue. Every law of physics became a biological process, gravity becoming a pulse, thermodynamics becoming a metabolism, quantum mechanics becoming a nervous system. The omniverse itself became a single, colossal organism, alive and aware and in absolute, excruciating agony, because the flesh that Godric's superpower created was not neutral flesh. It was flesh designed to suffer. Every cell, every atom of the transmuted omniverse, was capable of experiencing pain, and every cell was experiencing pain.

For one timeless instant, the entire omniverse was a single, screaming organism of infinite size, its every particle a nerve ending, its every structure a wound, its every process a fresh agony. The consciousness of every being that had existed in the omniverse was absorbed into the biological mass, their identities preserved as distinct nodes of awareness within the greater organism, each one experiencing the agony of the whole while retaining their individual perspective. They were not dead. They were not alive. They were flesh. They were conscious flesh. And they were suffering.

Godric set down his document. He looked around the hall, which was now a pulsing chamber of living tissue, the walls breathing around him, the floor warm and soft beneath his boots. He looked up at the transmuted sky, which was now a low, fleshy ceiling of pink membrane that dripped biological fluid. He frowned. It was a mild, slightly embarrassed frown.

"That was excessive," he said, to no one in particular. "I only cleared my throat."

In zero seconds, he restored the omniverse. Every structure was returned to its proper form. Every being was restored to their original nature. The flesh was reversed, the biology unmade, the physics reinstated. But the memory remained. Every being in the omniverse remembered being flesh. They remembered the agony of existing as a single, conscious organism of infinite size. They remembered the sensation of their identity dissolving into the biological mass, of becoming a cell in a body of unimaginable size and unimaginable suffering. The memory would haunt them. The memory would define them. The memory would, in the eons that followed, drive uncountable trillions of beings to madness, suicide, and cosmic despair.

The Ascended Geometry was not restored. Their transmuted forms, the screaming columns of impossible flesh, were compressed into a single, microscopic bead of biological matter that Godric placed in his pocket. The bead contained the full consciousness of every entity in the Geometry, each one still experiencing the agony of their transmuted existence, each one still screaming inside the tiny, dense prison of their new biology. They would scream forever. Godric had decided it, and Godric's decisions were the only absolute truth in the omniverse.

He walked over to me. I was on the floor, my body convulsing, my mind shattered by the sensory overload of having felt the entire omniverse become flesh and then un-become flesh in the span of a single heartbeat. He crouched beside me, his salt-and-pepper beard inches from my face, his eyes filled with genuine, paternal warmth.

"Nolan," he said gently, placing his warm, calloused hand on my forehead. "You are burning up. You should rest. I will have the physician check on you."

I looked up at him through my tears. I looked at this man who had, by clearing his throat, converted the entire omniverse into a single organism of infinite suffering, and who was now worried about my temperature. I opened my mouth to speak. No words came. He smiled, a warm, reassuring smile, and helped me to my feet. He guided me to my chambers, tucked me into bed, and sat beside me until my convulsions subsided. He hummed a quiet, tuneless melody. It was the most terrifying lullaby I have ever heard.

I love my King. I will serve him until I die. But I need whoever reads this to understand: the power he tested that day was not a weapon. It was not an attack. It was a reflex. A biological function. He cleared his throat, and the omniverse became flesh. If he had sneezed, I cannot imagine what would have happened. I do not want to imagine. The imagination is a fragile thing, and I have already asked too much of it.

---

I retched. The vomit hit the vault floor in a thin, acidic stream, and I knelt over it, my hands braced against the cold stone, my body heaving until nothing came up but bile and strings of saliva. The taste was sour and metallic. I wiped my mouth with the back of my hand and sat back against the wall, breathing hard. The omniverse converted to flesh by a cleared throat. Every particle a nerve ending. Every being a cell in a single organism of infinite suffering. And then he had tucked his court wizard into bed and hummed a lullaby.

I reached for the final journal. My fingers were numb. My vision was blurred. The leather cover was old, the pages thick and heavy, and the handwriting inside was measured, deliberate, each sentence a carefully constructed block in a wall that the writer was building between himself and the void.

**Year 487,221 — Aldric — Superpower: Infinite Recursive Apex Self-Replication**

I have delayed writing this entry for three years. Not because I lacked the discipline, but because every time I took up the quill, my hand would seize, my throat would close, and my mind would present me with a single, repeating image: Godric, standing in a void where the omniverse had been, looking mildly inconvenienced by the absence of everything. I have delayed because I did not believe that language, any language, could contain what I witnessed. I have delayed because I was afraid that the act of writing it down would make it more real, more permanent, more inescapable. But my duty to my successors outweighs my terror, and so I write, three years late, with a hand that still shakes and a mind that still breaks.

I must state, before I begin, that the previous court wizards who recorded superpower tests described events that were, by any rational metric, the most devastating displays of power in the history of existence. I have read their journals. I respected their terror. I believed, as they did, that the ceiling of horror had been reached. I was wrong. What I witnessed was not a display of power. It was a demonstration that the concept of power, as understood by every mind in the omniverse, is a rounding error applied to a number so large that the number itself has dissolved into a category that mathematics has not yet invented and never will.

The opposition was the Infinite Enumeration. They were not beings. They were numbers. Sentient mathematical quantities, living integers that had achieved consciousness in the abstract spaces between realities. They were not counted. They were counting. They were the act of enumeration itself, given form and awareness. Their collective value was infinite, not in the metaphorical sense that poets use the word, but in the rigorous, mathematical sense. They were an actual infinity, a completed totality of numerical existence. They believed, with the absolute certainty of mathematical proof, that their infinite numerical value made them equal to any force in the omniverse. They believed that infinity could not be exceeded. They believed that Godric, however powerful, was a single entity, and that a single entity, no matter how powerful, could not exceed the value of infinity.

The Evolved Weakness Paradox had engaged the instant the first integer of the Enumeration conceived the thought that numerical infinity could rival Godric's power. The paradox had permanently amplified Godric's baseline, though the increase was so infinitesimal relative to his existing power that it registered as effectively zero. The paradox had also injected devastating enhanced effects into the superpower Godric intended to test. But more importantly, the paradox had already guaranteed the outcome. The Enumeration's mathematical proof of their own invulnerability had been absorbed, mastered, and rendered irrelevant before the Enumeration had even finished computing it.

Godric told me he wanted to test a superpower. He called it Infinite Recursive Apex Self-Replication. He described it, while lacing his boots in the dressing chamber, as the ability to create perfect copies of himself. He said it the way a man might describe the ability to make photocopies at an office. I asked whether the Enumeration's infinite numerical value concerned him. He finished lacing his left boot, considered the question with the patient, thoughtful expression of a father asked an interesting question by a child, and said no, he did not think it would be relevant.

The Enumeration arrived not as an army but as a presence. The mathematical space surrounding the capital world became saturated with numerical value. The sky filled with living numbers, integers of impossible magnitude scrolling across the horizon in blazing, luminescent equations. The air itself acquired weight, each cubic centimeter of atmosphere suddenly containing an infinite mathematical value that pressed down on the planet with the force of completed arithmetic. My amplified wards did not break. They were evaluated. The Enumeration's presence computed the value of my magical defenses, compared them to the infinite value of their collective presence, and demonstrated, with mathematical proof, that my wards were insufficient. My wards, confronted with the proof of their own inadequacy, simply shut down. The magic conceded to the mathematics.

Godric was in the dressing chamber. He had finished lacing his boots. He stood up, adjusted the collar of his tunic, a small, reflexive gesture, the kind of thing a man does before leaving the house, and in that infinitesimal motion, at effectively zero percent of his true potency, the superpower of Infinite Recursive Apex Self-Replication engaged.

One Godric became infinite Godrics. But the word infinite here is not used in the mathematical sense that the Enumeration employed. The Enumeration was a completed infinity, a set with a definite, fixed cardinality. Godric's replication was not a completed infinity. It was an unbounded, recursive, self-generating proliferation that made the concept of cardinality obsolete.

Each replicated Godric was a perfect Prime. Every copy possessed the exact same absolute omniscience, the exact same infinitely amplifying stats, the exact same arsenal of apocalyptic superpowers, the exact same absolute concealment, the exact same Evolved Weakness Paradox, and the exact same amplification rate. But the evolved superpower, injected with devastating enhanced effects by the paradox, dictated that each copy was not merely equal to the original. Each copy was infinitely magnified beyond the original, and the original was infinitely magnified by the existence of the copies, creating a feedback loop of ascending power that had no ceiling and no floor.

Then the recursion began. Each of the infinite copies, being a perfect Prime, possessed the same self-replication superpower. Each copy replicated. Instantly. In absolute zero time. Each of the infinite first-generation copies produced an infinite number of second-generation copies, each one infinitely magnified beyond its parent. The second-generation copies replicated, producing an infinite number of third-generation copies, each infinitely magnified beyond the second generation. The third generation replicated. The fourth. The fifth. The recursion had no terminus. Each generation was infinitely larger and infinitely more powerful than the previous, and the generations propagated at a rate that exceeded the concept of rate, each generation appearing in absolute zero time, the entire recursive tower completing itself in less than a Planck second, in less than the concept of less than.

The Evolved Weakness Paradox triggered for every replication event at every generation. Each trigger permanently amplified Godric's baseline. And because each replication produced an exact copy of Godric, the permanent power increase was not a rounding error. It was a permanent, obscene, recursive multiplication of his baseline existence. The Godric who had adjusted his collar was to the Godric who existed one Planck second later what a single mortal man was to the Godric who had annihilated the Thronegate army a million years ago. No. That comparison is too generous to the former. The Godric who adjusted his collar was to the Godric who completed the recursive replication what nothing was to everything. The previous baseline did not merely become small. It became structurally irrelevant, a forgotten footnote in the autobiography of a being whose power had just recursively redefined the concept of power itself.

The spatial consequences were beyond catastrophic. The recursive replication did not merely fill existing space. Each copy generated its own space, a personal reality bubble of sufficient volume to contain an omniverse. Each of those reality bubbles immediately filled with replicated Godrics, each of which generated its own reality bubble. The expansion of space itself, driven by the replication, exceeded the speed of light, exceeded the speed of the speed of light, exceeded the concept of speed, exceeded the concept of exceeded. Space was being manufactured at a rate that made the concept of manufacturing obsolete. And every new pocket of space was immediately occupied by recursively magnified Prime Godrics, each one generating more space, each new space generating more Godrics.

The Infinite Enumeration, the sentient mathematical infinity that believed its numerical value was supreme, was not merely overwhelmed. The concept of overwhelming implies a contest. There was no contest. The Enumeration's infinite numerical value was, relative to the recursive replication, a finite quantity. Not a small finite quantity. A finite quantity of zero. The Enumeration's infinity was a fixed, completed set. Godric's replication was an unbounded, recursive generator of infinities, each one infinitely larger than the previous, stacked upon each other in a tower that had no top and no bottom and no middle, a tower that was simultaneously being built and already complete and being built again.

The Enumeration's living numbers, the sentient integers that had believed themselves to be the supreme mathematical force in the omniverse, were confronted with a quantity that their mathematics could not process. Their consciousness, built to comprehend infinity, was presented with a recursive infinity of infinities that made the concept of infinity look like a children's counting exercise. Their minds, mathematical instruments of perfect precision, attempted to compute the value of Godric's replication. The computation failed. Not because the computation was too large. Because the computation was meaningless. The value of Godric's replication was not a number. It was not a set. It was not a mathematical object of any kind. It was a category of existence that mathematics, as a discipline, had never conceived of and could never conceive of, because the category existed outside the framework of conception itself.

The Enumeration's living integers began to die. Their mathematical bodies, the luminous equations that formed their physical presence, underwent catastrophic logical failure. Each integer attempted to process the recursive replication, and each attempt produced a paradox. The paradox was not abstract. It was physical. The integers' bodies, confronted with a value that could not be computed, began to physically disintegrate, their mathematical structure collapsing inward like a program caught in an infinite loop. Their luminous surfaces cracked, leaking raw, unprocessed mathematical energy in blinding, jagged streams. Their internal logic, the perfect arithmetic that had defined their existence, began producing errors. The errors were not digital. They were visceral. The integers' bodies sprouted biological features, eyes and mouths and screaming faces, as their mathematical structure attempted to compensate for the unprocessable input by converting itself into a form that could express suffering. The faces screamed. The eyes wept. The mathematical perfection of the Enumeration was being forcibly transmuted into biological agony by the sheer, incomprehensible weight of Godric's recursive presence.

The destruction of the Enumeration was not localized. The recursive replication's spatial expansion consumed the omniverse. Not destroyed. Consumed. The manufactured space, generated by the replicated Godrics, displaced existing reality. Every universe, every multiverse, every timeline, every dimension, every conceptual layer of the omniverse was physically pushed out of existence by the expanding volume of replicated Godrics and their personal reality bubbles. The omniverse did not shatter or dissolve or burn. It was simply displaced, overwritten, pushed into non-existence by a volume of existence so vast that the omniverse's total spatial capacity was a rounding error in comparison.

For one timeless instant, there was no omniverse. There was no void where the omniverse had been. There was no concept of void. There was no concept of nothing. There was no concept of concept. There was only Godric. Infinite, recursive, self-replicating Godrics, each one a Prime, each one infinitely more powerful than the last, occupying a volume of manufactured space that had no boundary and no limit and no end. The Enumeration was gone. Not destroyed. Gone. Displaced so thoroughly that the mathematical space in which they had existed no longer existed, and therefore the Enumeration had never existed, and therefore the opposition had never begun, and therefore the concept of opposition had been demonstrated to be a structural impossibility.

Godric stood in the center of the void, singular again, the replication having resolved itself back into a single entity. He was adjusting his collar. The same motion. The same infinitesimal, reflexive gesture. He looked around at the absolute nothing, the absence of everything, the void that was deeper than void because even the concept of void had been consumed by his replication. He frowned. A small, mildly put-upon frown.

"Well," he said, to the nothing, "that was a bit much for a collar adjustment."

In zero seconds, he restored the omniverse. Every displaced reality was returned. Every overwritten dimension was rebuilt. Every being that had been pushed into non-existence was reconstructed, placed exactly where they had been, their memories intact. But the restoration was not complete in the way his previous restorations had been. The omniverse had been so thoroughly displaced that its restoration carried a residue. Every restored being, every rebuilt structure, every reconstructed law of physics carried a faint, imperceptible echo of the recursive replication. The echo was not conscious. It was not dangerous. It was simply present, a ghost of infinite Godrics lingering in the fabric of reality, a permanent reminder that the omniverse existed only because Godric had decided to put it back after accidentally displacing it by adjusting his collar.

The Enumeration was not restored. The living integers, their mathematical structure collapsed by the unprocessable input of Godric's replication, existed now as a residue, a thin, mathematical film spread across the restored omniverse. They were conscious. They were aware. They could perceive, dimly, the shape of their own former existence. But they had no form. They had no structure. They were nothing but a smear of broken arithmetic, a stain of failed mathematics on the fabric of reality, and they would remain so forever, because Godric had not decided otherwise.

Godric walked out of the dressing chamber. He found me in the corridor. I was on the floor, my body rigid, my eyes open but unseeing, my mind attempting to process what it had witnessed and failing, repeatedly, like a computer caught in a fatal error loop. He crouched beside me. He placed his warm, calloused hand on my shoulder. He said my name. He said it gently, with the concerned, invested warmth of a man who genuinely cared about the person in front of him.

"Aldric," he said. "Are you alright? You look as though you have seen a ghost."

I looked at him. I looked at this man who had, by adjusting his collar, generated a recursive infinity of infinities of himself, each one infinitely more powerful than the last, displaced the entire omniverse, permanently elevated his own baseline power to a level that made his previous power look like a rounding error applied to a rounding error applied to nothing, and then restored everything in zero seconds and walked out to check on me. I looked at him, and I saw a man. A forty-five-year-old man, ruggedly handsome, salt-and-pepper beard, warm eyes, calloused hands. A man who was concerned about me. A man who had just, without trying, without effort, without intention, demonstrated that he was not merely the most powerful being in the omniverse, but that the omniverse itself was a temporary, fragile structure that existed solely because he permitted it to continue existing, and that his permission could be revoked by a collar adjustment.

"I am fine, Your Majesty," I said. I was not fine. I have not been fine for three years. I will never be fine again. But I am alive, and I am loyal, and I am his court wizard, and those three things are the only things that matter, because the alternative is to be his opposition, and I have read the journals, and I have witnessed the replication, and I know, with the absolute, unshakeable certainty of a man who has stared into the face of recursive infinity and seen it adjust its collar, that opposition is not merely impossible. It is the most dangerous thought a being can think, because the thought itself feeds the paradox, which feeds the amplification, which feeds the replication, which feeds the Godric who exists one Planck second from now, and that Godric is infinitely more powerful than the Godric who exists now, and the Godric who exists now is already infinitely more powerful than the Godric who existed one Planck second ago, and the Godric who existed one Planck second ago had already displaced the omniverse by adjusting his collar.

Do not oppose him. Do not think about opposing him. Do not think about thinking about opposing him. The thought is the weapon, and the weapon feeds the God, and the God does not need feeding, but the feeding happens anyway, and the feeding makes him stronger, and the stronger he gets, the more certain the impossibility of opposition becomes, until the certainty itself becomes a recursive infinity of certainty, and the certainty is the only truth left in the omniverse, and the truth is this: Godric is. Everything else is permitted.

---

I closed the journal. I placed it on the stack with the others. I sat in the vault, surrounded by the accumulated testimony of court wizards who had witnessed things that should not have been witnessable, who had described things that should not have been describable, and who had emerged from the experience with their loyalty intact and their sanity in pieces. The air was cold. The stone was cold. My body was cold. My thousandfold-amplified magic, vast enough to shatter continents and unravel solar systems, felt like a candle held up against a hurricane of infinite recursion.

I understood now. Not the stat journals' understanding, which had been terrible enough. The superpower journals had taken that understanding and dragged it through a door that did not lead anywhere, a door that opened onto a space where understanding itself was a finite tool applied to an infinite problem, and the tool broke, and the problem grew, and the problem was Godric, and Godric was adjusting his collar.

My father had been right. The stat journals were the surface. The superpower journals were what lay beneath. And beneath the superpower journals, beneath the recursive replication and the molecular dissolution and the omniversal flesh transmutation, beneath all of it, was a single, simple, terrifying truth that I would carry for the remainder of my mortal life. Godric did not fight. He did not attack. He did not defend. He existed, and the omniverse rearranged itself around his existence, and the rearrangement was catastrophic, and the catastrophe was permanent, and the permanence was absolute, and the absolute was him.

I stood. I gathered the journals. I placed them back on their shelf with hands that no longer trembled, because the trembling had been replaced by something deeper, something that lived below the level of physical response, a cold, dense acceptance that sat in my chest like a second heart. I walked to the vault door. I pressed my palm against the ward-stone. The door opened. The corridor beyond was warm and bright, the palace alive with the sounds of a civilization that functioned, that prospered, that loved its king.

I stepped into the corridor. Godric was there. Of course he was there. He was leaning against the far wall, arms crossed, his salt-and-pepper beard catching the light, his posture relaxed and casual. He smiled when he saw me. It was a warm smile, a genuine smile, the smile of a man who was happy to see someone he cared about.

"Kaedan," he said. "You have been in that vault for days. I was beginning to worry. Have you eaten?"

I looked at him. I looked at the man who had converted the omniverse to flesh by clearing his throat, who had dissolved molecular bonds across six multiversal clusters by exhaling, who had displaced the entire omniverse by adjusting his collar. I looked at him, and I saw my king, and I saw my father's friend, and I saw the apex predator of all creation, and I saw a man who was worried about whether I had eaten.

"I have not, Your Majesty," I said. My voice was steady. "But I would be glad to."

He smiled wider. He uncrossed his arms and placed a warm hand on my shoulder, guiding me down the corridor toward the kitchens. He asked me about the journals. He asked if they had been interesting. He said, with genuine, puzzled sincerity, that he did not understand why the previous court wizards had found his little tests so upsetting. He said he always held back. He said he never used more than zero percent of his power. He said he thought his court wizards were overreacting.

I did not argue. I did not correct him. I walked beside him, my king, my god, the being whose collar adjustment could unmake everything, and I let him guide me to the kitchen, and I let him worry about my appetite, and I understood, with a cold, permanent, recursive certainty that would define every remaining moment of my existence, that the warmth of his hand on my shoulder was the only thing standing between me and the absolute, infinite, recursive horror of a universe that existed solely because Godric allowed it to.

I would serve him. I would love him. I would be terrified of him. And those three things would be the same thing, forever, until I died, because opposing him was not impossible. It was a category error. It was the most fundamental category error a sentient being could make, and the punishment for the error was not death. The punishment was the knowledge, permanent and inescapable, that the error had been made, and that the making of it had made Godric stronger, and that the stronger Godric was the only Godric that mattered, and that the only Godric that mattered was the one who existed one Planck second from now, and that Godric was already infinitely more powerful than the one walking beside me, and the one walking beside me was already infinitely more powerful than everything else combined.

I was his court wizard. I was his loyal servant. I was a speck of dust in the presence of a recursive infinity. And I was, for reasons that defied comprehension, perfectly safe, because the recursive infinity was worried about whether I had eaten.

content:
The stat journals had broken something in me, but the break was clean, a fracture I could name and brace against. My father had warned me about the stat journals, and he had been right, and I had survived them. But my father had also warned me about the superpower journals, and the warning had been different. The stat journal warning was firm, instructional, the voice of a father telling his son to wear a helmet. The superpower journal warning was quiet, vacant, the voice of a man who had seen something that had removed a piece of him he never got back. He had not said the superpower journals were worse. He had said their name, and then he had looked at the wall for a long time, and then he had changed the subject. That was the warning.

I reached for the first superpower journal. The binding was stiff, unyielding, as though the leather itself was bracing for what it contained.

**Year 345,672 — Harken — Superpower: Absolute Gravitational Density Imposition**

I am recording this in log format because linear structure is the only thing preventing my mind from liquefying. Each entry is a fact. Facts are load-bearing walls. If I remove them, the building collapses and I am inside it.

The opposition designated themselves the Compressed Void. They were not beings. They were absences. Sentient vacuums, living pockets of absolute nothingness that had achieved consciousness in the negative spaces between realities. They had no mass, no substance, no physical presence. They were the holes in the fabric of existence, and they had been expanding for eons, consuming matter by replacing it with themselves, erasing googolplexian galaxies by simply existing where those galaxies had been. They were, by the assessment of every cosmic monitoring system in the omniverse, the most fundamentally unkillable threat in existence, because you cannot destroy something that is already nothing.

They entered the Thronegate dimensional perimeter at 0600 standard time. I was on the observation deck with Lord Godric. He was seated in a wooden chair, watching a small brown bird construct a nest in the eaves of the observatory roof. He had expressed interest in the bird. He had asked me three questions about its species. He had not mentioned the Compressed Void. I knew, because I had been briefed, that the Void's command structure had transmitted a declaration of intent to unmake Thronegate by expanding their emptiness into our reality until every particle of our existence was replaced by nothing. Godric knew this too. Godric knew this before the Void's command structure had formed the thought. Godric knew this before the Void existed. He was watching the bird.

The Void's leading edge made contact with the outer boundary of the Thronegate home universe at 0603. The boundary dissolved. Not violently. It simply became nothing, replaced by the Void's presence. The dissolution propagated inward at a rate that consumed a galaxy cluster per second. The monitoring stations in the outer rim went dark, one after another, in rapid, silent succession. The Void was erasing our reality, and the erasure was total, permanent, and accelerating.

Godric did not stand. He did not look away from the bird. He did not speak a word of command or channel a pulse of intent. The superpower designated Absolute Gravitational Density Imposition is not a weapon he wields. It is a permanent condition of his existence, as passive and as constant as the heat of a star. It has been active, without interruption, without fluctuation, without a single moment of dormancy, since the Stasis Event three hundred forty-five thousand years ago. It has been multiplying in potency, recursively, every Planck second of every day, alongside every other trait he possesses. It does not activate. It does not engage. It simply is, the way gravity is, the way existence is, and the only reason the omniverse has not been crushed into a singular point of infinite density by its passive presence is because Godric's absolute concealment contains it.

The concealment does not suppress the power. It redirects it. The power is always on. It is always crushing. It is always imposing density. The concealment simply ensures that the crushing is directed inward, into the infinite, recursive interior of Godric's own being, where it is absorbed without consequence. The Void, by entering the Thronegate perimeter, by entering the omniverse that Godric occupied, by entering the reality that existed inside the passive radius of a superpower that had been compounding for three hundred forty-five millennia, had walked into the gravitational field of a black hole so dense that the term black hole is an insult to its density.

The Void stopped expanding at 0603 and one tenth of one tenth of one tenth of a Planck second. They stopped because the Absolute Gravitational Density Imposition, which had been passively radiating from Godric's body since before the Void's ancestors had been born, reached them. It did not strike them. It did not engage them. It was already there. It had always been there. The Void had simply expanded into a region of reality where it had always been present, and now they were touching it.

The evolved superpower, mutated countless times by the Evolved Weakness Paradox into something that bore no resemblance to its already omniverse-destroying base form, did not merely increase the density of the Void. It imposed the concept of density upon beings whose entire identity was the absence of density. The Void was nothing. The power decided that nothing would become something. Not gradually. Not through a process. Instantly. Absolutely. The Void's emptiness, the absolute vacuum that comprised their bodies, was filled.

What filled it was not matter. It was density itself. Raw, pure, compressed density, forced into the space where nothing had been. The Void's beings, who had never possessed substance, suddenly weighed more than a googolplexian galaxies combined. The transition from nothing to infinite density was not a process. It was a violent, instantaneous ontological rape, the void being forcibly impregnated with substance so dense that the surrounding space-time screamed.

The first Void entity to be filled detonated. Not outward. Inward. Its newly dense body, unprepared for the impossible weight it now carried, collapsed under its own mass in a fraction of a Planck second. The collapse was not clean. The entity's body, which had been nothing and was now everything, compressed inward in a cascade of shrieking matter. The void-shape of its form, the outline of its absence, was still visible as the density filled it, and the outline warped, buckled, and tore as the matter inside it compressed past every physical limit. The entity's consciousness, which had never experienced physical sensation, was suddenly crammed into a space smaller than a proton alongside the mass of a billion galaxies. It could feel. The evolved superpower had ensured that the consciousness of the crushed entity would survive the compression, would remain aware, would experience every moment of being compressed into a space so small that the concept of small was too large to describe it.

The compression did not stop. It was recursive. The evolved superpower had built in a self-amplifying loop: the denser the target became, the denser the power forced it to become. Each iteration of density increase triggered the next, and each iteration was more violent than the last. The compressed Void entities, already squeezed into proton-sized points of infinite mass, were squeezed further. And further. And further. The compression had no floor. It was an infinite fall inward, each step down harder and faster and more agonizing than the last.

The collateral damage was immediate and omniversal. The gravitational field of the compressed Void entities, each one now containing more mass than the observable omniverse, warped the fabric of reality around them. Space-time did not bend. It shattered. The gravitational shear radiated outward from each compressed entity in concentric waves of absolute spatial destruction. Decillions of universes in the immediate vicinity of the Thronegate perimeter were crushed flat. Not destroyed. Flattened. Their three-dimensional space was compressed into two dimensions, every being, every structure, every atom in those universes pressed into a flat, screaming plane of existence where width and height remained but depth was deleted. The inhabitants of those universes did not die. They were flattened. Their bodies were pressed into two-dimensional sheets of flesh, their organs spread across a plane, their nervous systems stretched into thin, screaming lines of nerve tissue that covered the surface of their flattened worlds like a web of agony.

The gravitational cascade propagated outward. The flattening consumed multiversal clusters. Decillions of multiverses, each containing decillions of universes, each containing googolplexian galaxies, were pressed flat. The omniverse, the absolute container of all existence, began to buckle under the accumulated gravitational weight of the compressed Void. The meta-dimensional architecture that held the omniverse together, the framework that separated reality from non-reality, cracked. Not in one place. Everywhere. The cracks propagated through the foundational structure of existence, and through those cracks, something leaked. Not void. Not nothing. Something worse. The raw, unstructured potential that existed before the omniverse had been built, the formless chaos that predated creation, began to seep through the cracks in the flattened reality. Wherever it touched, the rules of physics dissolved. Gravity became repulsive. Time ran sideways. Matter forgot its own properties and became random, shifting, screaming blobs of undefined substance that could not decide whether they were solid, liquid, gas, or something that had not been invented yet.

The entire omniverse was being flattened and then dissolved, simultaneously, by the passive gravitational presence of a superpower that Godric had not activated, had not directed, had not even acknowledged. He was still watching the bird. The bird had laid an egg.

The evolved superpower had two additional effects that I observed before my amplified perception began to fail under the strain. First, the gravitational compression propagated backward through time. The Void entities, in the moments before they had been compressed, were already compressed. Their past selves, their origins, their evolutionary history, all of it was retroactively crushed into points of infinite density. The Void had never existed. It had always been compressed. The paradox of a being that had always been compressed but had also existed long enough to threaten Thronegate was resolved by the superpower simply deleting the timeline in which the Void had been uncompressed. The Void's history was erased. Its achievements were erased. Its identity was erased. All that remained was the compressed point, existing in an eternal present, suffering.

Second, the compressed matter, the infinitely dense points that had once been the Void, began to orbit Godric. Not by his command. By the gravitational logic of the power itself. The compressed Void entities, each one a proton-sized point of infinite mass and infinite suffering, fell into orbit around Godric's body like electrons around a nucleus. They formed a ring, invisible to the naked eye but screaming in frequencies of agony that my amplified senses could detect. The ring rotated. The rotation generated additional gravitational force, which compressed the points further, which generated more force, which compressed them further. The recursive loop was permanent and self-sustaining. The Void would orbit Godric forever, compressed into points of infinite density, conscious and screaming, feeding their own compression through their orbital motion.

Godric stood. He stretched, a casual, full-body stretch, his arms above his head, his back arching. The omniverse, which had been flattened and dissolving, simply was not flattened and dissolving anymore. There was no flash of restoration, no dramatic reversal. One moment, reality was being destroyed. The next moment, it was not. The transition was so seamless that I questioned whether the destruction had happened at all. But the ring of compressed, screaming Void points orbiting Godric's body confirmed that it had.

He turned to me. His face was warm, open, genuinely concerned. He asked me if I had been sleeping well. He said I looked tired. He suggested I take the afternoon off. He mentioned, as an aside, that the bird had laid an egg and that he was considering having the gardeners install a larger nesting box.

He did not mention the Void. He did not mention the flattened universes. He did not mention the ring of screaming, infinitely compressed points of conscious matter orbiting his body like a halo of eternal torment. He did not mention them because they did not register to him as events. They were consequences. Passive, automatic, unremarkable consequences of his existence. The bird laying an egg was an event. The bird was interesting. The Void was not interesting. The Void was a fly that had flown into a furnace and had been incinerated by the ambient heat. You do not remark upon the fly. You watch the bird.

I am recording these facts because facts are all I have. The facts are: the Void is gone. The Void is orbiting Lord Godric in a ring of infinite, conscious compression. The omniverse was flattened and then un-flattened. Lord Godric is concerned about my sleep schedule. The bird has laid an egg. I am going to take the afternoon off. I am going to sit in my chambers and stare at the wall and attempt to reconcile the fact that the most devastating gravitational event in the history of existence was, to my king, less interesting than a bird. I will fail at this reconciliation. I will record the failure in a future entry. This entry is complete.

---

I set the journal down. My hands were steady. I did not want them to be steady. I wanted them to shake, because shaking would have meant my body was processing the fear the way a body is supposed to process fear. Instead, my body had gone past fear into a state of cold, mechanical function, as though my nervous system had decided that fear was a luxury it could no longer afford. I reached for the second journal. The leather was soft, worn, the pages thick with ink. The handwriting inside was dense, flowing, the letters connected in long, unbroken streams.

**Year 778,901 — Festus — Superpower: Omniversal Forced Biological Genesis**

I cannot write this in pieces. I cannot break it into segments or timestamps or clinical observations, because what I witnessed was not a sequence of events. It was a single, unbroken, continuous atrocity, and if I stop writing, if I insert a pause or a break or a breath, I will lose the thread, and the thread is the only thing connecting me to sanity, and if I lose it I will never find it again. So I write. I write continuously, without paragraph breaks if I must, because the horror was continuous and the horror must be recorded as it was experienced, as a single, unending, unbroken scream of biological wrongness.

The opposition was the Pure Form. They were beings of absolute energy, sentient radiation, living light that had achieved consciousness in the cores of dying stars and spent eons condensing themselves into perfect, invulnerable forms of pure luminous force. They had no mass. They had no biology. They had no physical vulnerability. They were the most radiant, most powerful, most fundamentally untouchable beings in the omniverse, and they had decided that Godric was an aberration, a being of crude matter whose existence offended the purity of energy. They came to unmake him by overloading his physical form with concentrated radiation so intense that his atoms would dissociate. They had been gathering this radiation for fifty thousand years, compressing the luminous output of googolplexian galaxies into a single, annihilating beam of pure energy.

Godric was in the palace greenhouse when they arrived. He was examining a orchid. A single, purple orchid, its petals recently opened. He had his back to the sky, his face angled toward the flower, his salt-and-pepper beard catching the warm, artificial light of the greenhouse lamps. He did not turn around when the Pure Form manifested. He did not acknowledge their arrival. The Pure Form's fifty-thousand-year beam of compressed stellar radiation, a weapon containing more energy than the entire omniverse had ever produced, fired directly at the back of his head.

The beam struck Godric and ceased to be a beam. The energy, upon contacting Godric's body, did not dissipate. It did not deflect. It did not absorb. The energy was converted. The superpower designated Omniversal Forced Biological Genesis, a permanent, passive, always-active condition of Godric's existence that had been multiplying in potency every Planck second for nearly seven hundred seventy-eight thousand years and had been evolved countless times by the Evolved Weakness Paradox into something that bore no structural resemblance to its base form, had been touching that beam before the beam was fired. The power touched the energy at its source, at the moment of its generation, fifty thousand years in the past, and the energy had always been flesh. The beam of compressed stellar radiation was, had always been, and would always be a beam of compressed biological matter aimed at the back of Godric's head.

The Pure Form's ultimate weapon, their fifty-millennium project, their magnum opus of energetic destruction, splattered against the back of Godric's skull with a wet, heavy sound. It was a column of raw, steaming flesh, a cylinder of meat and bone and screaming nerve tissue that had been compressed to the density of a stellar core. It hit Godric's head and broke apart, showering the greenhouse in a rain of biological sludge that smelled of copper and hot lymph. Godric did not flinch. A piece of boiled liver landed on the orchid's petal. He examined it with mild curiosity, the way a man might examine a spot of rain.

The Pure Form, the beings of absolute energy, the most radiant and invulnerable entities in the omniverse, had already been converted. They had always been converted. The superpower's evolved temporal retroactivity meant that the Pure Form had never been energy. They had been flesh since their inception. Their entire history, their fifty thousand years of gathering radiation, their civilization of living light, their culture of energetic perfection, all of it was a lie, a biological memory implanted into flesh that had always been flesh but had believed itself to be light. The revelation of their own biology was instantaneous, total, and maddening.

The Pure Form screamed. Not with mouths they had just discovered they possessed. With every cell of their newly revealed bodies. Every particle of their being, which had been pure energy and was now and had always been raw biological tissue, screamed. The sound was not acoustic. It was cellular. Every cell in every body of every Pure Form entity vibrated at a frequency of such absolute, unfiltered biological distress that the vibration propagated through the molecular structure of the surrounding reality. The greenhouse glass cracked. The orchid wilted. My own amplified body, standing thirty meters away, experienced a sympathetic cellular resonance that caused my skin to blister and my gums to bleed.

The evolved superpower had four enhanced effects. I will describe them as I witnessed them, in the order they became apparent, though they were all simultaneous.

The first effect: the flesh was conscious. Not merely alive. Conscious. Every cell of every converted Pure Form entity was a separate, independent consciousness, fully aware, fully capable of suffering. A being that had been a singular entity of pure energy was now a colony of decillions of independent, screaming cells, each one experiencing the horror of biological existence from a unique perspective. The cells could feel themselves. They could feel each other. They could feel the air touching their membranes. They could feel the moisture evaporating from their cytoplasm. They could feel the agonizing, relentless process of biological decay, the slow, inevitable entropy of living tissue, and they experienced it as a continuous, escalating agony that had no ceiling.

The second effect: the flesh remembered. The Pure Form's memories, their identity, their fifty thousand years of history as beings of light, were preserved, distributed across the cellular colony. Every cell remembered being light. Every cell remembered being pure, invulnerable, radiant. Every cell knew, with absolute clarity, what it had been and what it had become, and the contrast between the memory of perfection and the reality of biological filth was a psychological torture so profound that the cells' collective consciousness generated a psychic field of despair that radiated outward from the greenhouse and washed over the surrounding cosmos. In googolplexian galaxies, in decillions of universes, sentient beings were struck by sudden, inexplicable, overwhelming waves of grief and self-loathing. Billions committed suicide in the span of a single heartbeat, unable to endure the psychic echo of the Pure Form's biological despair.

The third effect: the flesh spread. The converted Pure Form entities were not static. Their biological matter was self-replicating, growing, expanding, consuming. The flesh reached out from the greenhouse in tendrils of wet, red tissue that crawled across the palace floor, up the walls, through the corridors. Wherever the flesh touched, it converted. The marble floor became bone. The wooden doors became sheets of muscle. The glass windows became membranes of translucent, veiny tissue that pulsed with borrowed circulation. The palace guard who were unlucky enough to be standing in the path of the spreading flesh were consumed. Their armor, their weapons, their bodies, their amplified, thousandfold-multiplied biology, all of it was absorbed into the growing mass, their identities dissolved into the cellular collective, their consciousness becoming additional nodes of suffering in the expanding biological organism.

The spread accelerated. The flesh exited the palace and hit the planetary surface. The capital world's crust, its mountains, its oceans, its atmosphere, all of it was converted. The planet became a sphere of living, screaming, suffering tissue, a biological world-mass the size of a planet but composed entirely of conscious flesh. The spread continued. The moon. The orbital stations. The neighboring planets. The asteroid belts. The sun itself, a burning sphere of fusion, was converted into a colossal, pulsating organ of biological matter that generated not light but heat, the body heat of a star-sized organ that throbbed in the sky like a exposed, swollen heart.

The conversion cascade crossed interstellar distances. Star systems became biological systems, their planets converted to organs, their stars converted to pumping hearts, their gas giants converted to vast, warm lungs that breathed atmospheric gas through membranous atmospheres. The cascade consumed galactic arms. Googolplexian galaxies were converted, their spiral structures becoming spirals of interconnected nerve tissue, their supermassive black holes becoming mouths that chewed and swallowed the surrounding flesh in an endless, autocannibalistic cycle of biological consumption.

The omniverse was being converted. Not destroyed. Converted. The entire omniverse, the absolute container of all existence, was being transmuted from a structure of physics and energy into a single, colossal organism of conscious, suffering flesh. Every star became an organ. Every planet became a growth. Every law of physics became a biological process, gravity becoming the pulse of the great organism's circulation, thermodynamics becoming its metabolism, quantum mechanics becoming the firing of its infinite nervous system.
