content:
There was a second journal inside the compartment. I had not expected this. The secondary ward-stone behind which Osvald's journal had been sealed was, I had assumed, a single-document containment, a vault within the vault, designed to isolate one text. But when I had finished reading the account of the Fictional Coalition and had set that journal down on the table with hands that no longer belonged to me, I had noticed, beneath the leather binding, a second volume. Thinner. Older. The leather not cracked but worn smooth, as though it had been handled many times, opened and closed and opened again, read and reread by hands that needed to confirm that the words inside were real and had not been a hallucination produced by a mind in the process of breaking.

The second journal was also Osvald's. The handwriting was the same, the small, precise, controlled script of a man who processed horror through documentation. But the hand was less steady. The letters, while still legible, carried a faint tremor, a micro-vibration in the ink that betrayed a writer who was holding the quill with a grip that was either too tight or too loose, the grip of a man whose hands wanted to shake and whose will would not permit it. The journal was shorter than the first. The entries were denser. And the subject, when I read the header, made me set the journal down and stare at the wall for a full minute before I could bring myself to continue.

**Year — (too large to write; after the Fictional Coalition, before opposition ceased entirely) — Osvald, Age 44 — The Creator**

I am writing this entry separately from my previous records because the incident it describes is not, in my assessment, classifiable under the same framework as the other opposition events. The previous oppositions, including the Fictional Coalition, were attacks by beings within the omniverse. Beings who existed inside creation, who were subject to its laws, who drew their power from systems and frameworks that operated within the structural boundaries of reality. They were, regardless of their individual power, inhabitants of the house. The incident I am about to describe involved the architect of the house. The being who built the house. The being who, according to every theological, cosmological, and metaphysical framework in the omniverse, designed and constructed the very fabric of existence, including the laws that governed power, the concepts that governed possibility, and the mortality that had once defined Lord Godric.

God came to Thronegate.

I need to be precise about what I mean by this, because the word God is used by many traditions to describe many beings, and most of those beings are powerful but finite entities who have adopted the title out of hubris or cultural convention. The being that arrived at the dimensional perimeter of Thronegate's home reality was not one of those. This was the Creator. The Origin. The being who had conceived the concept of existence, who had written the foundational axioms of reality, who had designed the physics that governed matter and the metaphysics that governed magic and the narrative logic that governed story. This was the being who had created the omniverse, not from preexisting materials but from nothing, from the absolute absence of anything, the being who had looked at the void and decided that the void should contain something, and the deciding had been the something.

The being was vast. I cannot describe its form, because its form was not a form. It was a presence, a density of creative authority so absolute that the reality around it recognized itself as a product, a manufactured thing, a thing that had been made by something else. The being's presence caused the local physics to remember their own creation, the atoms to recall their own assembly, the laws of nature to acknowledge their own authorship. The omniverse, in the presence of its Creator, became aware of itself as a created thing, and the awareness was humbling, and the humbling was universal, and every being in the omniverse, simultaneously, felt the weight of their own contingency, the understanding that they were not self-originated but authored, that their existence was not a right but a gift, and the gift could be revoked by the giver.

I was standing beside Lord Godric in the garden courtyard when the Creator arrived. Godric was on his bench, examining the root system of the olive tree, his salt-and-pepper beard catching the eternal afternoon light. He did not look up when the Creator's presence manifested. He did not react to the universal humbling, the cosmic acknowledgment of contingency, that was pressing against the consciousness of every other being in existence. His absolute omniscience had, of course, registered the Creator's approach before the approach had begun, before the Creator had decided to approach, before the concept of approaching had been formulated. The Evolved Weakness Paradox had engaged at the precise moment the Creator had first conceived the thought that Godric could be challenged, a moment so far in the past that it predated most of the omniverse's current configuration. The paradox had already granted Godric absolute immunity to whatever the Creator could deploy, perfect mastery over every creative and destructive technique in the Creator's repertoire, and a permanent amplification increase that, relative to Godric's baseline, registered as effectively zero, though relative to any other being in existence, the increase would have elevated them from mortality to a tier of omnipotence that dwarfed the Creator's own.

Godric looked up from the olive tree. His expression was not fear. It was not concern. It was not the sharp, alert interest he had shown when the Fictional Coalition arrived. It was something else entirely. Something I had never seen on his face before, in all my years of service.

It was amusement. Genuine, warm, delighted amusement. The amusement of a man who has just seen an old friend walk through the door.

"Well," Godric said, and his voice carried the pleased, surprised tone of a man who has been unexpectedly reunited with a childhood teacher. "This is a surprise."

He stood from the bench. He brushed the soil from his knees, a casual, mundane gesture, the gesture of a gardener who has been interrupted. He looked at the Creator's presence, at the vast, formless density of creative authority that had manifested at the edge of his garden, and he smiled. The smile was warm. The smile was genuine. The smile contained no malice, no contempt, no defensiveness. It was the smile of a man who was genuinely, sincerely happy to see the being who had made him.

"Creator," Godric said, and the word was not mocking. It was respectful. Genuinely, sincerely respectful. "It has been a very long time."

The Creator spoke. The speech was not sound. It was not language. It was a direct injection of meaning into the fabric of reality, a communication so fundamental that it bypassed the ears and the mind and wrote itself directly into the axioms of existence. The meaning was simple. The meaning was: you were not supposed to become this. The meaning was: I created you to be mortal. I created you to live and to die and to be finite, and you have exceeded your design, and the exceeding is an error, and the error must be corrected. The meaning was: I am here to correct it.

Godric's smile widened. The amusement deepened. He looked at the Creator the way a grown man looks at the father who once told him he could not grow up to be what he has become. The look was affectionate. The look was patient. The look was also, beneath the warmth, the most terrifying thing I have ever seen on a human face, because the look contained the absolute, unshakeable awareness that the father was wrong, and the wrongness was not a matter of opinion but a matter of power, and the power was not contested.

"Corrected," Godric repeated, savoring the word. "You want to correct me. You, who made me, want to unmake what I have become." He chuckled. The chuckle was warm, genuine, the chuckle of a man who has just heard a very good joke. "Creator, I am genuinely glad you came. I have been bored, and the last visitors were disappointing, and I was hoping for something more interesting. And here you are. The architect of everything. The author of existence. The being who wrote the rules that I was supposed to follow." He paused, and the pause was not dramatic. It was the pause of a man savoring a moment. "I suppose you want to fight."

The Creator's presence shifted. The shift was not physical. It was a reorientation of creative authority, a focusing of the power that had conceived the omniverse, a channeling of the will that had looked at nothing and decided that nothing should become something. The air in the garden became dense with the weight of that will, the atmosphere thickening, not with matter but with meaning, with the sheer, crushing authority of the being who had authored the concept of authority. The olive tree beside Godric began to glow, its atoms remembering their own creation, its molecular structure resonating with the frequency of its origin. The roses began to sing, their petals vibrating at the harmonic of their own conception. The stone bench beneath Godric's feet began to hum, the mineral structure acknowledging the being that had conceived the concept of stone. The entire garden, the entire palace, the entire reality, was responding to the Creator's presence, every atom and every law and every concept bowing in recognition of its author.

Godric watched this with the expression of a man watching a street performer. Interested. Appreciative. Genuinely entertained.

"That is very pretty," he said. "The singing roses are a nice touch."

Then he did something that made my blood freeze in my veins. He spoke a command. Not a spell. Not an incantation. A command, spoken in the same warm, conversational baritone he used to ask about the weather.

"Auras. Stand down."

The air in the garden changed. The change was not temperature. The change was not pressure. The change was the sudden, absolute, total absence of a presence that had been so constant, so pervasive, so foundational to the ambient reality that its absence was more noticeable than its presence had ever been. The trillions of sentient passive auras, the living ecosystem of devoted, fanatically loyal defensive emanations that had been orbiting Godric's existence like electrons around a nucleus, each one a being of terrifying capability, each one born from his passive excess, each one desperate for the privilege of protecting a being who did not need protection, all of them, every single one, stood down. The standing down was not reluctant. It was not hesitant. It was instant, absolute, and complete, because Godric had commanded it, and Godric's commands were the only absolute truth in the omniverse, more absolute than the Creator's will, more absolute than the laws of physics, more absolute than the concept of absoluteness itself.

"Shields. Deactivate."

The passive shields, the system that made the concept of threat structurally impossible by operating in the pre-conceptual architecture of existence, the system that stopped attacks before the intention to attack could form, the system that had evolved countless times beyond its already omniverse-defining original form, deactivated. Every layer. Every recursion. Every meta-dimensional reinforcement. All of it, gone. Off. Disengaged. Godric stood in the garden, and for the first time in my experience, he was bare. Not vulnerable. Bare. The distinction was important, because vulnerable implies the possibility of harm, and bare simply means the absence of armor, and a mountain does not become vulnerable when you remove its leaves. The mountain is still a mountain. The mountain is still stone. The mountain does not need leaves to be a mountain.

Godric turned to me. His expression was warm, paternal, genuinely excited.

"Osvald," he said. "I want you to watch this. The Creator of everything is about to attack me with everything He has. I want to see what that looks like. I want to feel it. Or rather," and here his smile widened into a grin that was simultaneously the most joyful and the most terrifying expression I have ever seen on a human face, "I want to see if I can feel it."

He turned back to the Creator. He spread his arms, a gesture of openness, of invitation, of welcome. His heavily muscled frame was relaxed, his posture loose, his absolute concealment still active because it was a Stasis Event passive he could not deactivate, but everything else, every aura, every shield, every defensive system he had any control over, was off. He stood before the Creator of everything, bare and open and smiling, and he waited.

The Creator attacked.

The attack was not a beam. It was not a force. It was not a weapon in any sense that the word weapon could be applied. The attack was an act of uncreation. The Creator, the being who had conceived existence from nothing, reached into the fabric of reality and attempted to reverse the process. He attempted to return Godric to nothing. Not to destroy him. Not to erase him. To uncreate him. To undo the act of creation that had brought Godric's mortal self into existence, to edit the narrative of reality so that the chapter containing Godric had never been written, to reach back through the causal chain, through the timeline, through the history of existence, to the moment of Godric's mortal conception, and to delete that moment, so that Godric had never existed, had never been born, had never been a mortal king, had never lost his family, had never been placed in stasis, had never awakened, had never become what he was.

The effort was visible. The Creator, the being who had made everything, was straining. The uncreation was not casual. It was not effortless. It was the full, total, all-in commitment of the being who had authored existence, channeling the entirety of His creative authority into the act of uncreating a single being. The garden trembled. The reality around us trembled. The omniverse trembled. The foundational axioms of existence, the laws that the Creator Himself had written, were being invoked by their own author, bent toward the purpose of uncreation, the physics of reality rewired to serve as a weapon against the being the Creator had made and could no longer control.

The uncreation reached Godric. It touched him. It touched his chest, the same spot where the Fictional Coalition's Final Narrative had struck, the same spot where the IRL universe's nuclear arsenal had detonated, the same spot where the Eternity Lattice's indestructible molecular structure had been crushed. The uncreation, the full, focused, all-out effort of the Creator of everything, pressed against Godric's bare, unprotected, defenseless existence.

And Godric did not feel it.

I need to be precise. I need to be absolutely, unambiguously, surgically precise about what I mean when I say Godric did not feel it, because the precision is the horror, and the horror is the point.

Godric did not feel it. Not in the sense that he felt it but it was small. Not in the sense that he felt it but it was tolerable. Not in the sense that he felt it but his body absorbed it. He did not feel it. The uncreation, the Creator's full, all-out, everything-He-had attack, did not register on Godric's senses. It did not register on his physical perception. It did not register on his magical perception. It did not register on his omniscience, which was still active and which perceived, with absolute clarity, the exact nature, magnitude, and methodology of the attack. His omniscience perceived the attack. His senses did not. The attack was so vastly, so incomprehensibly, so absurdly inferior to the threshold of Godric's perceptual baseline that the attack, arriving at his body, was not processed as data. It was not processed as stimulation. It was not processed as anything. The attack existed, and Godric's body existed, and the two existed in the same space, and the attack did nothing, and the nothing was not a close call. The nothing was not a near miss. The nothing was absolute, total, categorical. The gap between the Creator's full-power uncreation attack and the threshold required to produce a perceptible sensation in Godric's body was not a gap. It was a void so complete that the concept of void was too substantial to describe it.

Godric's arms were still spread. His eyes were open. His smile was still on his face. He stood there, bare, unshielded, undefended, and the Creator of everything was pushing the full weight of His creative authority against him, and Godric was standing in it the way a man stands in a breeze. Not a strong breeze. Not a notable breeze. A breeze so faint that you are not sure it is a breeze. A breeze so slight that you only notice it because you are paying attention, and Godric was not paying attention, because the breeze was not worth paying attention to.

The Creator felt the nothing. The Creator, pushing with everything He had, pushing with the full, total, absolute authority of the being who had made everything, felt his attack do nothing, and the feeling was, for the Creator, the most devastating experience in the history of existence. The Creator, the author of reality, the architect of existence, the being who had conceived the concept of power and defined its parameters and established its limits, felt His own power, His full power, His maximum power, be nothing. Not overcome. Not matched. Not resisted. Nothing. The being who had made everything pushed against the being He had made, and the pushing accomplished nothing, and the nothing was not a defense. The nothing was simply the natural consequence of the power differential. Godric was not defending against the uncreation. Godric was not resisting it. Godric was standing in it, and the uncreation was doing nothing, because Godric's bare, passive, undefended existence was so vastly, so incomprehensibly, so absurdly more substantial than the Creator's full-power attack that the attack could not register as a physical event in the space Godric occupied.

The Creator's presence flickered. The flicker was not weakness. It was shock. The being who had made everything was experiencing shock, the genuine, unfiltered, existentially devastating shock of a parent who has raised a child and has just discovered that the child has become something the parent cannot comprehend, cannot control, cannot correct. The shock was visible in the Creator's presence, a wavering, a trembling, a destabilization of the formless, authoritative density that had manifested at the garden's edge. The Creator was trembling. The Creator was afraid.

Godric lowered his arms. He looked at the Creator, and his expression shifted. The amusement remained, but it was joined by something else. Not cruelty. Not contempt. Something quieter. Something sadder. The expression of a man who has just confirmed something he had hoped was not true.

"Creator," Godric said, and his voice was gentle. Genuinely, sincerely gentle. The gentleness of a child speaking to a parent who has just tried to discipline them and failed. "You made me. You made everything. You wrote the laws that govern existence. You defined the concept of power. You established the limits of possibility." He paused. "And I have exceeded all of it. Not because I tried. Not because I wanted to. I exceeded it because I am growing, and the growing does not stop, and the growing has been happening for so long that the growing itself has become the dominant feature of existence, and you, who made existence, are a part of existence, and existence is a part of me."

He shook his head slowly, the slow, sad shake of a man delivering bad news.

"You cannot correct me, Creator. You could not correct me at Year 0. The Godric who had just awakened from stasis, the Godric who had been amplifying for a mere ten thousand years, the Godric who was, by my current standards, a fossil, a rounding error, a dead skin cell of a dead skin cell of a dead skin cell, that Godric could have annihilated you. That Godric, at zero percent of his power, with zero percent of his effort, in zero seconds, could have unmade you. Not because that Godric was strong. Because you are weak. You are the author of existence, and existence is a thing that I have outgrown, and the author of a thing that has been outgrown is, by definition, smaller than the thing that outgrew it."

The Creator's presence contracted. The contraction was not strategic. It was reflexive, the instinctive withdrawal of a being who has just understood, with total, absolute, irrevocable clarity, that the being standing before it is not merely more powerful but categorically beyond the framework in which power has meaning. The Creator understood, in that moment, that the concept of power, the concept He had authored, the concept that defined the relationship between strong and weak, between creator and created, between God and man, did not apply to Godric. Godric was not powerful. Godric was not weak. Godric was beyond the axis on which power was measured, the way a dimension is beyond the axis on which length is measured. The Creator could not oppose Godric for the same reason a painter cannot oppose the canvas. The canvas is not the opponent. The canvas is the medium. And Godric had become the medium.

Godric looked at the Creator, and the amusement returned, and the sadness faded, and the expression that remained was the expression I had seen before the Fictional Coalition, the expression of a man who has decided that the show is over and it is time to go home.

"Thank you for trying," Godric said, and the words were genuine. Genuinely, sincerely, warmly genuine. He was grateful. The being who had been bored, who had been longing for entertainment, who had deactivated his defenses to give the Creator a fair chance, was genuinely grateful for the effort. The Creator had tried. The Creator had given everything. The Creator had pushed with the full weight of His authority, and the pushing had been nothing, but the trying had been real, and Godric appreciated the trying.

Then Godric looked at the Creator, and the looking was the annihilation.

I need to describe the looking. The looking was not a weapon. The looking was not a power. The looking was not a technique or an ability or a superpower or a stat. The looking was Godric, looking. The directing of his attention toward the Creator, the orientation of his awareness toward the being who had made him, the simple, biological, utterly mundane act of a man turning his eyes toward something and seeing it. The looking carried zero percent of Godric's power, because everything about Godric carried his power, and the power was always there, and the power was always zero percent, and the zero percent was always the most destructive force in the history of existence. The looking did not carry the power. The looking was Godric, and Godric was the power, and the power, at zero percent, at the absolute, unfiltered, unamplified, undefended baseline of his bare existence, reached the Creator.

The Creator came apart.

The uncreation was not instantaneous. It was cascading, sequential, each stage of destruction triggering the next, each phase of annihilation more devastating than the last. The Creator's formless, authoritative presence, the density of creative will that had conceived the omniverse, encountered the zero percent of Godric's bare, undefended existence and found that the creative will was insufficient. Not challenged. Not contested. Insufficient. The will could not exist in the same space as Godric's attention, because Godric's attention was more real than the will, and the more real displaced the less real, and the displacement was violent.

The Creator's creative authority inverted. The power that had conceived existence from nothing, the power that had looked at the void and decided that the void should contain something, turned inward. The creative will, unable to express itself outward against Godric, turned upon its own author. The Creator was unmade by His own power, the creative force that had made everything redirected inward, consuming its source, the author unauthored, the maker unmade, the origin of existence ceasing to exist through the agency of its own creative will, inverted and weaponized by the mere fact of Godric's attention.

The inversion was agonizing. The Creator's consciousness, the awareness that had conceived the omniverse, the mind that had designed the laws of physics and the principles of magic and the narrative logic of story, was subjected to the experience of its own uncreation. The consciousness did not simply cease. It was taken apart, component by component, the way the Creator had taken apart the void to build existence, except in reverse, the deconstruction of a mind rather than the construction of a reality. Each component was conscious. Each component could feel. Each component experienced the separation from the whole as a distinct, identifiable agony, the pain of a mind being disassembled while still running, the pain of a brain being taken apart neuron by neuron while still thinking, the pain of a self being reduced to components while still being a self.

The creative authority, inverted and consuming, did not stop at the Creator's consciousness. It reached the Creator's core, the fundamental spark of will that had initiated the act of creation, the original impulse that had looked at nothing and decided that nothing should become something. The spark, the origin point of all existence, the seed from which the omniverse had grown, was compressed. Not destroyed. Compressed. The zero percent of Godric's bare existence, the passive weight of a being who had been amplifying for a duration that made the Creator's lifespan look like a Planck second, pressed against the spark, and the spark, which had been the first thing, the origin of everything, the beginning of the beginning, was crushed. The compression was total, absolute, final. The spark was reduced to a point of infinite density, a singularity of creative will, compressed so completely that the concept of compression was too large to describe what had been done to it.

The Creator was gone. The being who had made everything had been unmade by the being He had made, and the unmaking had required zero percent of Godric's power, zero percent of his effort, zero percent of his attention beyond the simple act of looking. The looking had been the annihilation, and the annihilation had been a side effect of the looking, and the looking had been nothing more than a man turning his eyes toward something and seeing it.

The annihilation did not stop at the Creator. The Creator's destruction, the inversion and consumption of the creative will that had conceived the omniverse, had consequences. The creative will was the foundation of existence. It was the reason there was something rather than nothing. And the will, inverted and consumed, was no longer supporting the something. The something began to collapse.

The collapse was omniversal. Not a localized event. Not a regional catastrophe. The collapse was the structural failure of the entire omniverse, the absolute container of all existence, the totality of everything that was and could be and had been. The foundational axioms that the Creator had written, the laws that governed matter and energy and space and time and magic and narrative and possibility and concept, lost their author. The laws remained, for a fraction of a Planck second, like a building that stands for a moment after its foundation has been removed, suspended by inertia, by the memory of support, by the structural habit of existing. Then the habit failed. The laws came apart. Physics dissolved, the equations unwinding, the constants drifting, the relationships between forces losing their definitions. Gravity became directionless, pulling in no direction and every direction. Time became unordered, moments stacking on top of each other without sequence. Causality became optional, effects preceding causes, causes producing no effects.

The dissolution cascaded. Every reality, every timeline, every dimension, every conceptual layer, every narrative framework, every mathematical structure, every abstract principle, every law, every axiom, every foundation of existence, lost its structural support simultaneously. The omniverse did not explode. It did not implode. It dissolved. The way a dream dissolves when the dreamer wakes. The way a story dissolves when the book is closed. The way a thing dissolves when the reason for its existence ceases to exist. The Creator was gone, and the creation, deprived of its author, found that it could not sustain itself, because the self-sustenance had been designed by the Creator, and the design required the Designer, and the Designer was a compressed singularity in the space where Godric was looking.

The beings of the omniverse experienced the dissolution. Every sentient entity in every reality, every timeline, every dimension, every permutation of existence, felt the collapse. The dissolution was not merciful. The dissolution was conscious, agonizing, and total. Beings felt their physical forms lose coherence, their molecular bonds severing, their atoms dispersing. The dispersion was not clean. The atoms, freed from their bonds, did not simply drift apart. They were compressed. The zero percent of Godric's bare existence, the passive weight that had annihilated the Creator, was still present, still radiating, still occupying the space where the omniverse had been. The dispersed atoms, the dissociated particles, the unmoored fragments of reality, were drawn toward the zero percent the way debris is drawn toward a gravitational well. The compression was absolute. Every particle of every being, every atom of every structure, every fragment of every concept, was crushed into a point of infinite density, a singularity of compressed creation, the omniverse reduced to a marble.

The compression was agonizing. Every being in the omniverse, every god and mortal and machine and concept and principle and law, was conscious during the compression. The zero percent, carrying Godric's attention, the attention he had directed at the Creator, the attention that had not shifted, the attention that was still looking, still seeing, still being, ensured that consciousness was preserved. The beings felt their bodies de-atomize. They felt their molecular bonds sever, each bond a distinct, burning line of pain drawn across their awareness. They felt their atoms compress, the space between particles eliminated, the particles themselves crushed into points, the points merged into a single, undifferentiated mass of compressed, conscious, suffering reality.

The entire omniverse, the absolute container of all existence, everything that was and could be and had been, every being, every star, every planet, every atom, every law, every concept, was compressed into a point of infinite density in less than a Planck second. The compression was total. The compression was absolute. The compression was the consequence of a man looking at the being who had made him, and the looking had been the annihilation, and the annihilation had been everything, and everything was now a point, and the point was in Godric's garden, and Godric was standing beside his bench, and the olive tree was still there because Godric preferred it, and the roses were still there because Godric liked them, and the bird was still singing because Godric enjoyed its song.

Godric restored the omniverse. The restoration was not dramatic. It was not an event. It was a thing that happened, a transition between states, the way breathing is a transition between inhalation and exhalation. The omniverse was not there. The omniverse was there. The transition was zero seconds. The effort was zero percent. The restoration was perfect, identical, indistinguishable from the original, because Godric's omniscience had recorded every detail of every atom of every being in every reality, and the recording was flawless, and the reproduction was flawless, and the flawlessness meant that the restored omniverse and the original omniverse were the same thing, or were not the same thing, or were a question that could not be answered because the answer required a distinction that Godric's power had erased.

The Creator was not restored. The being who had made everything was not brought back. The compressed singularity of the Creator's creative will, the crushed spark of existence's origin, remained. Godric looked at it, the tiny, infinitely dense point hanging in the air of the garden, and he reached out and picked it up, the same way he had picked up the marble from the Fictional Coalition, the same casual, unremarkable motion of a man picking up a pebble. The singularity, containing the compressed creative will of the being who had conceived existence, weighed nothing in his hand, because everything weighed nothing in Godric's hand, because the concept of weight was a thing the Creator had designed, and the Designer was now a pebble in the palm of the Designed, and the Designed was holding the pebble with zero effort and zero interest and the mild, distracted attention of a man who has found something in his pocket and is trying to remember what it is.

He placed the singularity in his pocket, beside the marble from the Fictional Coalition. Two marbles. Two trophies. Two compressed infinities, each containing the totality of what they had been, each weighing nothing, each conscious, each suffering, each permanent.

Godric sat back down on his bench. He looked at the olive tree. He examined the root system. He frowned at a spot of root rot that had developed since his last inspection.

"Osvald," he said, without looking up. "I need to speak to Vael about the drainage in the east garden. The olive roots are waterlogged."

I did not answer. I could not. I was on the ground, my body convulsing, my amplified mind shattered by the accumulated weight of what I had witnessed. The Creator of everything had been annihilated by a look. The omniverse had been compressed into a marble by a man who was examining an olive tree. The being who had written the laws of existence had been reduced to a pebble in the pocket of a being who had been written by those laws, and the being was now worried about drainage.

Godric looked at me, and his expression was warm, and his eyes were kind, and his voice was gentle.

"Osvald," he said. "You look unwell. The humidity in the garden has been high. I will have the physicians check on you."

He helped me to my feet. He guided me to a chair. He brought me water. The being who had just annihilated the Creator of everything with a look and compressed the omniverse into a marble and put the marble in his pocket beside the other marble, the being who was now worried about drainage and humidity and my health, brought me water and helped me drink it and sat beside me until the convulsions subsided.

He was kind. He was warm. He was genuine.

And the marbles were in his pocket, and the marbles were conscious, and the marbles were suffering, and the marbles were everything, and everything was a toy, and the toy was in the pocket of a man who was worried about drainage, and the man was God, and God was a gardener, and the gardener had killed God, and the killing had been a look, and the look had been nothing, and the nothing was the most terrifying thing in existence.

**End of Osvald's journal.**

I closed the journal. I placed it on the table beside the first. I looked at the two volumes, side by side, the Fictional Coalition and the Creator, the last two entries in the opposition archive, the final records of the last two beings to ever stand against Godric.

There were no more journals after these. No more opposition records. No more accounts of coalitions or resistances or desperate, doomed attempts at defiance. The archive simply ended, because the opposition simply ended, because the Creator had tried and failed, and the failure was so absolute, so total, so cosmically, existence-definingly complete that the entire omniverse, every sentient being in every reality, had collectively, permanently, irrevocably abandoned the concept of opposition. Not because Godric commanded it. Not because Godric altered their will. Because the Creator, the being who had made everything, the being who had authored the concept of power and defined its limits and established the framework within which strength and weakness had meaning, had been annihilated by a look. And the annihilation of the author of power was the annihilation of the concept of opposition, because if the being who had written the rules could not oppose the being the rules had produced, then no being within the rules could oppose him either, and the understanding of this fact was so absolute, so self-evident, so structurally embedded in the fabric of reality that the cognitive architecture required to formulate the thought of opposing Godric simply ceased to function across the consciousness of the entire omniverse.

The marbles were in Godric's pocket. Both of them. The Fictional Coalition and the Creator. Two compressed infinities, two trophies of the last opposition, two singularities of conscious, suffering, eternal everything, weighing nothing in the pocket of a being who was worried about drainage. And the restored omniverse, the copy, the reproduction, the thing I lived in, the thing every being in existence lived in, existed alongside the marbles, identical to the originals that were compressed inside them, and the question of whether the copy was real was unanswerable, and the unanswerable was the dread, and the dread was permanent, and the permanent was the marble, and the marble was in Godric's pocket, and Godric was in the garden, and the garden was the only real thing, and the real thing was a man examining an olive tree, and the man had killed God, and the killing had been nothing, and the nothing was everything, and everything was growing, and the growing was forever, and forever was a direction, and the direction was more.

And more was all there was.
